pair.title: Apple fixes bug that cops used to extract deleted chat messages from iPhones

story.title: Apple fixes bug that cops used to extract deleted chat messages from iPhones | TechCrunch

comments.text: This is a textbook example of why we need baseline standards for law enforcement access to device data. Apple fixed the bug, sure, but the real issue is that there's no federal framework requiring tech companies to design systems that *prevent* this kind of unintended data retention in the first place. The FBI shouldn

kids.text:
> The FBI shouldn't have access to this data in the first place.

Apple fixed a symptom, not the problem. The real issue is that notification payloads shouldn't persist in any database after the originating message is deleted. This should be baked into iOS's notification architecture, not left to individual

I'm not sure the notification architecture fix alone solves this. iOS still caches notification content in multiple places (lockscreen, notification center history, crash logs). Unless Apple also implemented automatic scrubbing of those caches when an app marks data as deleted, you're just moving the problem around. Did the security

I'd be curious to see the actual notification retention logs from a real device. The 404 Media piece didn't include technical details on how long Apple's cache persists or under what conditions it gets wiped. Signal's request makes sense, but "up to a month" is a pretty wide window-did

This reads like someone who discovered encryption in 2015. We had this exact fight in the 90s with Clipper Chip, key escrow, the whole CALEA nightmare. Law enforcement will always find a backdoor or a side channel (like this notification cache bug), and then we'll have the same conversation again in five years.

The real problem isn't that Apple needs baseline standards. It's that the baseline standard is already written in the Fourth Amendment, but it's treated like a quaint suggestion. You need a warrant. That's the framework. It's just nobody enforces it anymore because "terrorism" and "think of the children" have a way of making people forget how that amendment works.

Apple fixing bugs is fine. But pretending that design review will solve this is fantasy. The FBI will find the

I think you're framing this as a policy problem when it's actually a design problem. Apple didn't need federal mandates to *not cache deleted message content*. They chose to, probably for notification performance reasons. Signal had to ask them to fix it.

The real lesson isn't "we need baseline

This is exactly why some of the secure messaging apps built their own notification handling. Wire, for instance, doesn't rely on the OS notification stack for sensitive content at all. They render a generic "new message" ping instead. It's more work upfront but avoids this whole class of bugs.

You're right that this exposes a deeper design gap. We should be thinking about notification retention as a security boundary, not an implementation detail. The fix is necessary but it's really just patching the symptom.

comments.text: The notification caching issue is real, but Signal users should know this was always a potential vector. iOS notifications are stored in NotificationCenter's database, which forensic tools have had access to for years. The fix Apple deployed (iOS 18.5.1) clears notification payloads after 24

kids.text: This reminds me of the broader issue with notification databases across mobile OSes. Android has similar problems with notification history in the system logs. I spent a few months last year helping forensic examiners understand what they could actually extract, and the answer was "way more than users think." Even after you delete an app, notification fragments can persist in logcat buffers or the notification shade database for surprisingly long periods. The real fix isn't

This is exactly the kind of security theater Apple loves. They fix the notification cache but leave a dozen other vectors untouched. Meanwhile, the real problem-that forensic tools have unrestricted access to device internals at all-goes unaddressed. Until Apple actually encrypts the entire notification database end-to-end (which they won't, because iCloud syncing), this is just

comments.text:
This is interesting timing. We've been building encrypted messaging infrastructure for a few years now, and the notification caching issue is something we discovered independently about 6 months ago when stress-testing our own app against forensic tools.

The broader problem is that iOS treats notifications as a separate security boundary from the app itself. Even if Signal deletes a message immediately, the OS can cache that notification content in the unified notification database with its own retention policies. Apple's fix here helps, but it's really just patching the symptom.

What we ended up doing was requesting that messaging apps be able to suppress notification content entirely (show only "[1 new message]" with no preview), and then have the OS enforce immediate deletion of notification data when the parent message is deleted. Signal should be able to do this already, but it requires explicit app-level hooks that iOS doesn't fully expose.

If anyone

kids.text: This reminds me of the late 90s when every OS vendor kept inventing new ways notifications could leak sensitive data. We had this same problem with macOS and AOL Instant Messenger cached transcripts, Windows and Outlook preview panes writing to unencrypted temp files. Took years of

comments.text: This is just security theater. Apple knew about this for years and only fixed it when 404 Media embarrassed them into it. How many iPhones are still running the old version? Millions. And cops will keep using this exploit anyway because there's zero enforcement.

kids.text: I work with law enforcement on digital forensics, and this actually reflects a real problem we see across the board. The notification cache issue isn't unique to Apple-Android has similar quirks with how it stores notification metadata. The frustrating part is that once these things are public, the fix rolls out, but adoption lags. We still see devices from 2023 in the field that haven't updated. And yeah, there's definitely a question about whether

This reminds me of the broader issue with notification databases that nobody really talks about. iOS keeps a ton of metadata around notifications for things like notification center, Siri suggestions, and analytics. Even on Android, you've got similar caching in the notification shade. The real problem isn't just Signal or Apple-it's that every messaging app has to trust the OS notification system, and the OS treats that as

Android's notification shade doesn't persist that data nearly as long. iOS keeps notification content in the notificationcenter.db for 30 days by default, whereas Android's NotificationHistoryDatabase typically clears after 24 hours. That's the actual gap here.
